text: Spatial multi-omics has helped address the limitations of single-cell sequencing, which often leads to the loss of spatial context among cell populations. Integrated analysis of the genome, transcriptome, proteome, metabolome, and epigenome has enhanced our understanding of cell biology and the molecular diseases. Moreover, this approach offers profound insights into the interactions between intracellular and intercellular molecular mechanisms involved in the development, physiology, and pathogenesis of human diseases. In this comprehensive review, we examine current advancements in multi-omics technologies, focusing on their evolution and refinement over the past decade, including improvements in throughput and resolution, modality integration, and accuracy. We also discuss the pivotal contributions of spatial multi-omics in revealing spatial heterogeneity, constructing detailed spatial atlases, deciphering spatial crosstalk in tumor immunology, and advancing translational research and cancer therapy through precise spatial mapping.

Single-cell sequencing has been instrumental in providing detailed insights into gene expression at the individual cell level for decades. This technique has revealed the complexity of cellular diversity, exacerbated by processes such as cell proliferation, differentiation, and death, particularly in relation to the local and distant environment of the cell. Single-cell sequencing can detect cellular heterogeneity, enabling detailed analysis of individual cell behavior, mechanisms, and relationships. However, despite these advantages, single-cell sequencing often fails to retain critical spatial information about cell populations, resulting in the loss of crucial spatial context. To overcome this limitation, spatial multi-omics has emerged as a transformative technology, enabling the precise localization of cells within tissues and the quantitative measurement of gene expression in situ. This advancement marks an important technological breakthrough in life sciences and biomedicine, with wide-ranging applications in neuroscience, developmental biology, and cancer research. Furthermore, spatial multi-omics allows researchers to investigate the development of multicellular organisms from single totipotent cells, as well as their function, aging, and disease progression. High-throughput multi-omics technologies, such as genomics, epigenomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics, have also facilitated the mapping of diverse molecular layers, significantly broadening the scope of biological analysis and our understanding of complex biological systems.

section: Technologies for Spatial Omics - Spatial Transcriptomics
text: Spatial transcriptomics has significantly enhanced our understanding of cellular organization and intra-tissue interactions based on the systematic measurement of gene expression levels across tissue space. Recent advancements have focused on increasing the number of detectable genes or proteins, enhancing sensitivity and resolution, simplifying operation, and expanding the size of the analyzed area. Fundamental spatial transcriptomics technology has the ability to reveal the precise spatial localization of RNA molecules within tissues. Mainstream strategies include image-based in situ transcriptomics and oligonucleotide-based spatial barcoding followed by next-generation sequencing (NGS). Image-based methods primarily include fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) and in situ sequencing (ISS). Single-molecule imaging and multiplexed error-robust FISH (MERFISH) address optical crowding by reducing optical crowding, albeit at the cost of increased imaging rounds and time. Sequential FISH (seqFISH) addresses optical crowding by decreasing the number of transcripts per image, requiring additional imaging rounds. Despite these advancements, smFISH is limited by the spectral overlap of fluorophores. In contrast, ISS-based methods like STARmap employ dynamic annealing and ligation (SEDAL) to reduce sequencing errors and enable high multiplexing and analysis of thicker tissue slices. BaristaSeq, an optimized padlock probe-based technique, significantly enhances amplification efficiency and sequencing accuracy.

section: Technologies for Spatial Omics - Spatial Genomics & Proteomics
text: Spatial genomics examines modifications to the DNA sequence and chromatin structure. Techniques like In Situ Genome Sequencing (IGS) and Slide-DNA-seq enable spatially resolved sequencing of DNA from intact tissues. IGS expands non-targeted genomic samples in a natural spatial environment, creating an in situ sequencing library. Slide-DNA-seq generates a spatial index array of beads with unique DNA barcodes; tissue slices are transferred onto this array, and spatial barcoding is performed via photolysis, attaching proximal genome fragments to barcodes for PCR amplification and sequencing. Spatial proteomics involves the large-scale study of proteins. Two primary approaches are mass spectrometry (MS)-based methods and imaging-based methods. MS offers high sensitivity and resolution but involves complex sample preparation. Imaging methods provide high sensitivity but have low throughput. Deep visual proteomics (DVP) combines AI-driven image analysis with single-cell laser microdissection and ultra-high-sensitivity MS to associate protein abundance with complex cellular phenotypes while preserving spatial context.

section: Technologies for Spatial Omics - Spatial Epigenomics & Metabolomics
text: Spatial epigenomics examines epigenetic modifications like histone acetylation, methylation, and DNA methylation. Spatially resolved assays like CUT&Tag (Cleavage Under Targets and Tagmentation) enable the mapping of histone modifications or transcription factor binding sites with high signal-to-noise ratio. Spatial metabolomics involves the detection and quantification of metabolites. Targeted approaches (e.g., Fourier Transform Mass Spectrometry (FT-MS), Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)) focus on specific biochemical questions, while untargeted approaches (e.g., LC/MS) aim for global metabolite profiling. LC/MS is common for untargeted metabolomics but faces challenges from metabolite diversity and adduct formation. Computational tools like CAMERA help identify isotopes, adducts, and fragments. The integration of these technologies aims to create a comprehensive picture of the epigenome and metabolome in situ.

While single-cell multi-omics yields insights into gene regulation across omics layers, it lacks spatial information. Spatial multi-omics enables the simultaneous analysis of multiple data modalities (transcriptomics, proteomics, genomics, epigenomics, metabolomics) on the same tissue section. Key integration strategies include: 1) Integration of spatial transcriptomics and (epi)genomics: Technologies like Spatial ATAC-RNA-seq and Spatial CUT&Tag-RNA-seq use deterministic barcoding in tissue (DBiT) to co-map chromatin accessibility/histone modifications and mRNA expression. 2) Integration of spatial proteomics and transcriptomics: Platforms like Spatial Multi-Omics (SM-Omics) and Spatial-CITE-seq allow simultaneous profiling of epitopes and transcriptomes within single cells using DNA-barcoded antibodies. 3) Integration of spatial transcriptomics and metabolomics: Spatial multimodal analysis (SMA) combines spatially resolved transcriptomics and mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) to reveal associations between transcriptomes and metabolomes. 4) Integration of multiple modalities: Multi-omics in situ pairwise sequencing (MiP-seq) detects multiplexed DNA, RNA, proteins, and biomolecules at subcellular resolution using padlocking probes and a pairwise-sequencing strategy.

Spatial multi-omics is crucial for deciphering spatial-specific atlas production of molecular and cellular profiles in health and disease. For instance, MERFISH was used to construct a spatial atlas of the human middle and superior temporal gyrus, revealing differences between humans and mice. In cancer, spatial multi-omics decodes spatial-based heterogeneity. Studies in metastatic prostate cancer and non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) have revealed intratumor heterogeneity in androgen receptor (AR) activity, immune infiltration, and metabolic states. In gastric cancer, integration of spatial transcriptomics and metabolomics visualized intratumor metabolic heterogeneity and cell metabolic interactions, highlighting glutamine overutilization in tumor tissue. These applications demonstrate the power of spatial multi-omics in mapping tumor ecosystems and identifying region-specific molecular features.

Spatial multi-omics provides new perspectives on complex interactions within the tumor microenvironment (TME). By integrating mass spectrometry imaging-based spatial metabolomics and transcriptomics, researchers identified distinct interfaces at tumor-nerve junctions, such as peritumoral lymphoid tissue (PLT) with distinct metabolic profiles. Spatial trajectory and lineage tracking technologies are crucial for studying developmental trajectories. Spatial transcriptomics allows comprehensive transcriptomic analysis of thousands of cells, revealing spatiotemporal relationships and molecular mechanisms underlying differentiation. In therapeutic applications, spatial multi-omics helps investigate new therapies. For example, analysis of triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) revealed that microbial metabolites like TMAO induce pyroptosis and enhance CD8+ T cell immunity. In ulcerative colitis, spatial transcriptomics and proteomics identified mononuclear phagocytes as key cells impacted by anti-integrin therapy (Vedolizumab), showing changes in spatial distribution and cellular phenotypes.

The document includes Table 1 detailing sub-technologies of spatial transcriptomics, genomics, proteomics, epigenomics, and metabolomics, outlining their analyte principles, features, and limitations. For example, smFISH uses in situ hybridization of reverse-complementary oligo probes conjugated with fluorophores, offering precise localization but suffering from low probe specificity. Table 2 lists technologies for spatial multi-omics integration, such as Slide-DNA-seq (integrating transcriptomics and genomics with 25μm resolution), Spatial ATAC-RNA-seq (microchannel-based, 20-25μm resolution), and SM-Omics (integrating proteomics and transcriptomics with 100μm resolution). Table 3 summarizes applications in deciphering spatial-specific atlases (e.g., human maternal-fetal interface), decoding spatial heterogeneity in diseases (e.g., metastatic prostate cancer), analyzing spatial crosstalk in tumor immunology (e.g., gastric cancer), and investigating new therapies (e.g., TNBC, ulcerative colitis).

The field of spatial omics technologies aims for higher resolution, deeper coverage, greater multiplexity, and enhanced versatility. Future advancements include 3D reconstruction of larger tissue regions and comprehensive analysis of spatiotemporal multi-omics. Challenges remain in integrating datasets from different omics modalities due to differing spatial features, necessitating specialized hardware and software for visualization. Key steps include normalizing data matrices, removing low-quality data, and developing independent benchmarks for integration algorithms. The ultimate goal is to achieve multi-omics techniques with spatial single-cell resolution, revolutionizing our understanding of cell biology, disease mechanisms, and enabling personalized precision medicine. In conclusion, the integration of multi-omics with spatial analysis is a rapidly evolving field that promises deeper insights into complex biological systems, though it requires advanced computational and statistical methods to manage large-scale datasets and interpret spatial variations amidst technical noise.
